title: Patterns of Empire: The British and American Empires, 1688 to the Present By Julian Go Cambridge University Press. 2011. 304 pages. $99 paper
abstract: Soon after President Bush mistakenly declared the 2002 Iraq invasion as “mission accomplished,” and the U.S. military had to sink its wary boots into the uncertainty of endless war, the Atlanta Journal-Constitution commented that “the concept of America as a world empire, so controversial as to be almost unsayable just a few months ago, is now close to conventional wisdom” (Go:2-3). The United States as world empire? Unsayable? Steeped in a sophisticated and nuanced postcolonial understanding of modern empire, sociologist Julian Go, in his brilliant book, Patterns of Empire, researches the various dimensions to this substantive turn in world history, pursuing head-on the question of U.S. Empire, by asking: Is it real? Compared to what? These questions are typically eclipsed by the conventional explanation of American exceptionalism, that is, the U.S.'s actions are unique and incomparable. Moreover, without a feudal past, coveting no territory, the paragon of liberation and democracy, a colony once itself, the United States hardly qualifies as an empire, especially if empire is defined by the Dutch, Spanish or British legacies. From the U.S. vantage point, colonialism seemed to be an inefficient use of resources and power; the United States, having won the Cold War, inadvertently backed into its role as global hegemon, or so the legend goes.
